message.group-title: Intensive Care and Critical Care Medicine
message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>INTRODUCTION</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Hypoxemia in Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome due to Novel Coronavirus of 2019 (SARS-CoV-2) is mediated by severe inflammation that may be mitigated by corticosteroids. We evaluated pattern and effects of corticosteroid use in these patients during an early surge of the pandemic.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>METHODS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Observational study of 136 SARS-CoV-2 patients admitted to the Intensive care Unit between March 1 and April 27, 2020 at a tertiary care hospital in Indianapolis, USA.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Statistical comparison between cohorts and dosing pattern analysis was done. Outcome measures included number of patients requiring intubation, duration of mechanical ventilation, length of ICU stay and inpatient mortality.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>RESULTS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Of 136 patients, 72 (53%) received corticosteroids. Groups demographics: Age (60.5 vs. 65;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    .083), sex (47% male vs. 39% female;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    .338) and comorbidities were similar. Corticosteroid group had increased severity of illness: PaO2/FiO2 (113 vs. 130;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    .014) and SOFA (8 vs. 5.5;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    < .001). Overall mortality (21% vs. 30%;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    .234) or proportion of patients intubated (78 vs. 64%;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    .078) was similar. Mortality was similar among mechanically ventilated (27% vs. 15%;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    .151) however there were no deaths among patients who were not mechanically ventilated and received corticosteroids (0% vs. 57%;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    <.001). Early administration (within 48 hours) showed decrease in proportion of intubation (66% vs. 87 vs. 100%;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    .045), ICU days (6 vs., 16 vs. 18;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    <.001), and ventilator days (3 vs. 12 & 14;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    <.001). 45% received methylprednisolone.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>CONCLUSION</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Corticosteroids were used more frequently in SARS CoV-2 patients with higher severity of illness. Early administration of corticosteroids improved survival in non-mechanically ventilated patients; decreased ICU stay and may have prevented intubation.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Corticosteroid Use in Severely Hypoxemic COVID-19 Patients: An Observational Cohort Analysis of Dosing Patterns and Outcomes in the Early Phase of the Pandemic
